Generally, there are one or more patches to ﬁx a CVE. In

most cases, a CVE-ID corresponds to one git log, and a git log is identiﬁed by a commit-id. But sometimes there are more than one commit-id to ﬁx a CVE when there are several patches for the CVE. As long as the changed code (vulnerability ﬁxes, bug ﬁxes, etc.) is submitted, git logs will be created. Therefore, each patched version is uniquely identiﬁed by a commit-id or several commit-ids. We ﬁrst obtain the commit-ids corresponding to the CVE-ID, and then explore the changelog information. A changelog corresponds to the release of a new version, and it records all modiﬁcations to the previous version. Therefore, a changelog typically records many different git logs, so it is shorthand format for git logs. Although each change log is not completely consistent with the writing structure of a git log, there is no difference in key content information such as the commit-ids. If all commit-ids of a CVE are included in the changelog, it indicates that the CVE vulnerability has been patched in the current version . Thus, we use the commit-ids located by CVE-ID to traverse all changelogs available to obtain the committed (i.e., patched) version set. We regard them as the known patched versions .
As a running example, let us assume the three versions in

green dashed circles in Fig. 4 form the committed version set of this CVE-ID, in which V 3 . 0 . 6 and V 4 . 0 . 4 are the minimum and maximum committed version, respectively. However, not every branch corresponds to a committed version, and the reason will be introduced in Section IV-C .
B. Determine the Last Vulnerable Version of CVE-ID (R1)
As the maximum committed version recorded by the changel-
ogs, can V 4 . 0 . 4 be used as V upto of this CVE-ID? Is the same vulnerability deﬁnitely gone after V 4 . 0 . 4 ? The answer is no .
In this section, We will combine developer logs and patches to

determine V upto . To make it easier to understand, we ﬁrst give two examples to illustrate the problem. During our research, we ﬁnd an interesting phenomenon: starting from a certain version after the maximum committed version, the code may be restored to its previous unpatched state. Let us take CVE-2014-9715 as an example. The reason for its existence is that insufﬁciently large data type was used for certain extension data, which allows local userstocauseadenialofservice.Fromchangelogs,weknowthat itsmaximumcommittedversionis V 3 . 14 . 5 .However,weﬁndthat starting from V 4 . 12 , the code returned to the original unpatched state. According to the git log of CVE-2014-9715, this patch solves the denial of service vulnerability by reducing the size of the “ecache extension”. However,in its git log, the committers regarded that the previous patch released three years ago did not ﬁx this vulnerability well. Therefore, they restored the code to its unpatched state, and put forward the method of “compile-time assertion” to repatch the vulnerability. This illustrates the case that although the code is unpatched, the vulnerability corre- sponding to this CVE-ID no longer exists. In another example with CVE-2015-8558, unpatched code also reappeared in the

version after the maximum committed version. Similarly, from the description of the log information, the developers said that they used a new approach to patch the previous vulnerability, different from the ﬁrst patch.
Patches are developed and used to ﬁx bugs. However,

some patches are correct but do not perform as good as ex- pected(correct but not good), or do not ﬁx the bugs com- pletely(incomplete), or introduce new bugs(wrong). Conse- quently, the earlier committed patches may be totally unpatched or patially unptached, and then newer patches are commited (“repatch”). According to our study, the repathes is an inevitable issues through all versions of Linux Kernel and the number of repatches is signiﬁcant. In order to achieve high precision of identifying the affected versions, it is important to consider the phenomenon, otherwise, the errors are non-negligible and not acceptable. Our tool is based on the assumption of correctness of patches, so that we only consider repatches that the earlier patches are correct, then unpatched totally or partially, ﬁnnaly repatched with better patches. We do not consider wrong or incomplete patches.
For a given CVE-ID, patch-based detection [19] , [20] will

mistake the above repatch cases as “vulnerability reappearance”, so how do we verify whether the unpatched code has a real vulnerability? We already know that the information in the changelog is sometimes incomplete, so it is not enough to rely only on it. For example, for CVE-2018-21008, the NVD report shows that the last vulnerable version is 4.16.6, and the maxi- mum committed version based on the changelogs is 4.14.166, but there are still unpatched versions after 4.16.6. Our solution is to use both the patches and developer logs to accurately identify the unpatched code. In the case of repatch, our research found that it always includes the following two operations in the git log: (1) unpatch , consisting of complete unpatch and partial unpatch.Completeunpatchrestoresthepreviousunpatchedstate and changing the ‘-’ in diff hunk to ‘+’ and ‘+’ to ‘-’, which is an inverse process of patching. Patial unpatch is a partial inverse process of patching. It is similar to complete unpatch except that it only includes part of the diff hunk. (2) repatch , applying a new patch to the unpatched code.
We cannot easily locate the diff hunks of repatched code

through a CVE because a repatch is not released as a new CVE. Fortunately, the git log of a repatch clearly and accurately describes which previous patch(es) is(are) replaced by including the commmit-id of the previous git log in the title or the text body of the new git log for the repatch. We notice that the two operations of repatch, unpatch and repatch , may be carried out in one commit, resulting in a single git log. Compared with a previous patch, the location of a repatch may be the same code block in the same ﬁle, such as the repatch of CVE-2017-7477. It can also ﬁx code blocks in different locations of the same ﬁle, such as the repatch of CVE-2018-5873. It may also ﬁx code blocks in different ﬁles, such as the repatch of CVE-2014-9715. The two operations of repatch may also be carried out in two separate commits, resulting in two different git logs. The git log for the repatch operation can be located in the same git log list indexed by the same vulnerable ﬁle, the author, or the committer of the patch. Again, we can get the diff hunks of
